topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the otherwise mundane rhythm of my afternoon. My jaw tightened almost imperceptibly. I stared at the profile picture—a poorly lit selfie from a decade ago, featuring a smirk that used to make my stomach clench with dread. It was Mark. Mark, the architect of countless awkward moments and whispered humiliations in the seventh grade. A friend request. The sheer audacity of it made my fingers hover over the 'Decline' button, but a strange, heavy inertia kept me rooted to the spot. I felt a slow, simmering heat rise in my chest, a tight knot forming just beneath my ribs. I didn't even need to open the chat; the air around the device felt suddenly thick, charged with old resentment.

[story 2]

When the chime sounded, I didn't look up immediately. I was meticulously arranging my bookshelf, each volume placed with deliberate, precise alignment. Then the alert appeared. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that didn't quite reach my lungs. I finally glanced at the name: Mark. A muscle jumped in my cheek. I let out a quiet, humorless exhale, the sound barely audible over the distant traffic. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the icon. There was a distinct feeling of distaste washing over me, a cold slickness spreading through my limbs. I imagined the conversation—the forced pleasantries, the thinly veiled attempts at familiarity—and a faint, unpleasant tremor ran through my hands.

[story 3]

It was late, and I was scrolling aimlessly through social media when the notification popped up. My eyes scanned the screen, and the familiar name materialized: Mark. A slow, deliberate movement brought my gaze to the profile. A peculiar tightness settled in my throat, making swallowing feel like a chore. I didn't feel anger, not exactly; it was more like a deep, pervasive sense of being profoundly inconvenienced by an unwelcome intrusion. I minimized the app, leaning back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest. The silence of the room seemed to amplify the dull throb behind my eyes. I decided, with a quiet finality, that ignoring it entirely was the only appropriate response.
